title: Microfluidic systems and methods of use
abstract: Microfluidic channels networks and systems are provided. One network includes a first fluid channel having a first depth dimension; at least a second channel intersecting the first channel at a first intersection; at least a third channel in fluid communication with the first intersection, at least one of the first intersection and the third channel having a depth dimension that is greater than the first depth dimension. Also provided is a flow control system for directing fluids in the network. Systems are additionally provided for flowing disrupted particles into a droplet formation junction, whereby a portion of the disrupted particles or the contents thereof are encapsulated into one or more droplets. Further provided is a method for controlling filling of a microfluidic network by controlling passive valving microfluidic channel network features.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS  WHAT IS CLAIMED IS: 1. A microfluidic device, comprising: a first fluid channel having a first depth dimension; at least a second channel intersecting the first channel at a first intersection; at least a third channel in fluid communication with the first intersection, at least one of the first intersection and the third channel having a depth dimension that is greater than the first depth dimension. 2. The microfluidic device of claim 1, further comprising a fourth channel segment, fifth channel segment, sixth channel segment and seventh channel segment intersecting the fourth channel segment at a second intersection, the fifth, sixth and seventh channel segments being coplanar, and wherein a cross sectional dimension of the seventh channel segment perpendicular to the first plane is larger than the cross sectional dimension of the fourth channel segment perpendicular to the first plane 3. The microfluidic device of claim 2, further comprising one or more steps disposed within one or more of said fourth and seventh channel segments, wherein said one or more steps provide said cross sectional dimension of said seventh channel segment that is larger than said cross sectional dimension of said fourth channel segment. 4. The microfluidic device of claim 3, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least 1%. 5. A microfluidic system, comprising: a microfluidic channel network comprising first, second, third and fourth channel segments in fluid communication at a first intersection, the first, second, third and fourth channel segments being coplanar, and wherein a cross sectional dimension of the fourth channel segment perpendicular to the first plane is larger than a cross sectional dimension of the first channel segment perpendicular to the first plane; and a flow control system for directing a first fluid through the first channel segment into the first intersection and into the fourth channel segment, and directing one or more focusing fluids from the second and third channel segments into the first intersection and into the fourth channel segment. 6. The microfluidic system of claim 5, further comprising a fifth, sixth and seventh channel segment intersecting the fourth channel segment at a second intersection, the fifth, sixth and seventh channel segments being coplanar, and wherein a cross sectional dimension of the seventh channel segment perpendicular to the first plane is larger than the cross sectional dimension of the fourth channel segment perpendicular to the first plane; and 

 wherein the flow control system directs the first fluid and focusing fluids and second focusing fluids from the fifth and sixth channel segments into the second intersection. 7. The microfluidic system of claim 5, wherein the first fluid and focusing fluids flow in laminar flow into the fourth channel segment. 8. The microfluidic system of claim 5, further comprising one or more steps disposed within one or more of said channel segments and providing the larger cross sectional dimensions of said channel segments. 9. The microfluidic system of claim 8, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 1%. 10. The microfluidic system of claim 9, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 5%. 11. The microfluidic system of claim 10, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 10%. 12. The microfluidic system of claim 11, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 20%. 13. The microfluidic system of claim 12, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 30%. 14. The microfluidic system of claim 13, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 40%. 15. The microfluidic system of claim 14, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 50%. 16. The microfluidic system of claim 15, wherein the one or more step increases the cross sectional dimension perpendicular to the first plane by at least about 100%. 17. A microfluidic system, comprising: a microfluidic channel network comprising first, second and third channel segments in fluid communication at a first intersection, the first, second, and third channel segments being coplanar, and wherein a cross sectional dimension of the third channel segment perpendicular to the first plane is larger than a cross sectional dimension of the first channel segment perpendicular to the first plane; and a flow control system for directing a first fluid through the first channel segment into the first intersection and into the fourth channel segment, and directing a second fluid from the second channel segment into the first intersection and into the third channel segment. 18. The system of claim 17, further comprising a fifth, sixth and seventh channel segment intersecting the fourth channel segment at a second intersection, the fifth, sixth and seventh channel segments being coplanar, and wherein a cross sectional dimension of the seventh channel segment 

 perpendicular to the first plane is larger than the cross sectional dimension of the fourth channel segment perpendicular to the first plane; and wherein the flow control system directs the first fluid and focusing fluids and second focusing fluids from the fifth and sixth channel segments into the second intersection. 19. The system of claim 17 or claim 18, wherein the first fluid and focusing fluids flow in laminar flow into the fourth channel segment. 20. A microfluidic system, comprising: a first channel segment fluidly connecting a source of disruptable particles, with a first droplet forming junction, the first channel segment comprising a constricted region proximal to the droplet forming junction; and a flow control system for driving the disruptable particles through the constricted region, wherein the constricted region comprises a cross sectional dimension reduced sufficiently to induce disruption of the disruptable particles driven through the constricted region, and for flowing disrupted particles into the droplet formation junction, whereby at last a portion of the disrupted particles or the contents thereof are encapsulated into one or more droplets. 21. The system of claim 20, further comprising a second channel segment, a third channel segment and a fourth channel segment in fluid communication with said first channel segment, wherein said second channel segment, third channel segment and fourth channel segment facilitate formation of one or more droplets at said droplet forming junction. 22. The system of claim 21, further comprising a fifth, sixth and seventh channel segment intersecting the fourth channel segment at a second intersection, the fifth, sixth and seventh channel segments being coplanar, and wherein a cross sectional dimension of the seventh channel segment perpendicular to the first plane is larger than the cross sectional dimension of the fourth channel segment perpendicular to the first plane; and wherein the flow control system directs the first fluid and focusing fluids and second focusing fluids from the fifth and sixth channel segments into the second intersection. 23. The system of claim 20 or claim 21, wherein the first fluid and focusing fluids flow in laminar flow into the fourth channel segment. 24. The system of any one of claims 20, wherein said constricted region is positioned at a distance of fewer than 100 microns away from said droplet formation junction. 25. The system of any one of claims 24, wherein said constricted region is positioned at a distance of fewer than 50 microns away from said droplet formation junction. 26. The system of any one of claims 25, wherein said constricted region is positioned at a distance of fewer than 20 microns away from said droplet formation junction. 27. The system of any one of claims 26, wherein said constricted region is positioned at a distance of fewer than 10 microns away from said droplet formation junction. 28. The system of any one of claims 27, wherein said constricted region is positioned at a distance of fewer than 5 microns away from said droplet formation junction. 29. A method of co-partitioning particles using the system of claim 20, wherein said method comprises: providing one or more first particle and disrupting said particle by passage through said constriction; providing one or more second particle; and co-partitioning said first and second particle. 30. The method of claim 29, wherein said first particle is one or more cells and said second particle is one or more bead. 31. The method of claim 30, wherein said first particle is a single cell and said second particle is a single bead. 32. The method of claim 30 or claim 31, wherein said bead is a gel bead. 33. The method of any of claims 29-32, further comprising co-partitioning a barcode. 34. The method of claim 33, wherein said barcode is an oligonucleotide. 35. The method of claim 34, wherein said oligonucleotide is a plurality of oligonucleotides having the same sequence. 36. The method of any of claims 29-35, further comprising providing a lysing agent. 37. The method of any of claims 29-36, wherein said method is performed without addition of a lysing agent. 38. A method for controlling filing of a microfluidic network comprising: (a) providing a microfluidic channel network comprising a first channel segment and a second channel segment intersecting the first channel segment at a first junction; (b) providing a first fluid in the second channel segment up to the first junction, wherein capillary flow of the first fluid is interrupted at the first junction; (c) providing a second fluid in the first channel segment, wherein the second fluid is capable of controlling filling of the microfluidic channel network by releasing the interrupted flow of the first fluid into the microfluidic channel network; and (d) releasing the interrupted flow of the first fluid into the microfluidic channel network. 39. The method of claim 38, wherein said first channel segment comprises curved pinning points where the first channel segment meets the first junction. 40. The method of claim 38 or claim 39, wherein said curved pinning points are configured and arranged to provide the interruption of capillary flow of the first fluid. 41. The method of any one of claims 38-40, wherein the microfluidic channel network further comprises a third channel segment at the first junction, wherein the first fluid and second fluids flow in laminar flow into the third channel segment. 42. The method of any one of claims 38-41, wherein the second fluid comprises a surfactant. 43. The method of claim 42, wherein the surfactant concentration supports release of the interrupted capillary flow of the first fluid upon mixing of the first fluid and the second fluid. 44. The method of any one of claims 38-43, wherein the microfluidic channel network further comprises one or more additional channel segments intersecting the third channel segment at a second junction, and wherein the released capillary flow of the first fluid is interrupted at the second junction. 45. The method of claim 44, wherein the interruption of capillary flow of the first and second fluids at the second junction is the result of lower surfactant concentration in the mixed first fluid and second fluid. 46. The method of any one of claims 38-45, wherein the microfluidic channel network further comprises a channel expansion feature arranged and configured to control the rate of flow of the first fluid into the microfluidic channel network. 47. The method of claim 46, wherein the rate of flow of the first fluid is controlled to be reduced. 48. A method for controlling filing of a microfluidic network comprising: (a) providing a microfluidic channel network comprising a first channel segment and a second channel segment intersecting the first channel segment at a first junction; (b) providing a first fluid in the first channel segment up to the first junction, wherein capillary flow of the first fluid is interrupted at the first junction; (c) providing a second fluid in the second channel segment up to the first junction, wherein capillary flow of the second fluid is interrupted at the first junction; and (d) providing pressure to both the first and second channel segments to control filling of the microfluidic channel network by releasing the interrupted flow of the first and second fluids into the microfluidic channel network. 49. The method of claim 48, wherein the first channel segment comprises a first curved pinning point and the second channel segment comprises a second curved pinning point where the first and second channel segments meet the first junction. 50. The method of claim 48 or claim 49, wherein the curved pinning points are configured and arranged to provide the interruption of capillary flow of the first and second fluids. 51. The method of claim 48 or claim 49, wherein the first and second curved pinning points each comprise a step feature. 52. The method of claim 51, wherein the step features are configured and arranged to provide a smaller depth at the first junction compared to the depth of the first and second channel segments. 53. The method of any one of claims 48-52, wherein the microfluidic channel network further comprises a third channel segment at the first junction. 54. The method of any one of claims 48-53, wherein the microfluidic channel network further comprises one or more additional channel segments intersecting the third channel segment at a second junction, and wherein the released flow of the first and second fluids is interrupted at the second junction. 55. The method of any one of claims 48-54, further comprising a channel expansion feature arranged and configured to control the rate of flow of the first fluid. 56. The method of claim 55, wherein the rate of flow of the first fluid is reduced.
